questions.question: What is the key difference between a food allergy and a food intolerance?
options: An allergy involves the immune system and can be life-threatening, while an intolerance does not involve the immune system and is not usually life-threatening.
An allergy only ever causes mild stomach upset.
There is no real difference; the words mean exactly the same thing.
An intolerance is always more dangerous than an allergy.

questions.question: In a true food allergy, which part of the body wrongly reacts to a harmless food protein?
options: The muscles.
The digestive system only.
The immune system.
The skeleton.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common example of a food intolerance mentioned in the course?
options: Sesame allergy.
Lactose intolerance.
Shellfish allergy.
Peanut anaphylaxis.

questions.question: How quickly does an allergic reaction usually begin after contact with the allergen?
options: Only after several days.
Always exactly 24 hours later.
It never begins on the same day.
Quickly, usually within minutes to a couple of hours.

questions.question: Why does allergen awareness matter so much in a care setting?
options: Because allergies are never serious in older people.
Because some people rely on you completely and cannot check food, read labels, or clearly say what they react to.
Because care plans rarely mention food.
Because staff enjoy cooking elaborate meals.

questions.question: Which statement best describes your role in allergen awareness?
options: To prescribe medication for reactions.
To decide which allergies are genuine.
To diagnose allergies and give medical advice.
To know who has an allergy, follow their care plan, check food carefully and raise concerns straight away.

questions.question: How many allergens does UK food law identify as needing to be declared when used as ingredients?
Which of the following is one of the 14 named allergens that must be declared under UK law?
options: White sugar.
Table salt.
Fresh strawberries.
Sulphur dioxide and sulphites, preservatives found in some dried fruit, wine and soft drinks.

questions.question: Which of these is included among the 14 named allergens?
options: Potato.
Chicken.
Rice.
Celery.

questions.question: The course notes that allergens can hide in unexpected places. Which is an example it gives?
options: Salt in a savoury dish.
Mustard in a salad dressing.
Water in a glass.
Sugar in a dessert.

questions.question: On a prepacked food label, how must the 14 allergens be shown?
options: They only need to be mentioned if a customer asks in writing.
They can be left off the label completely.
They must be printed in a separate leaflet inside the packet.
They must be listed in the ingredients and emphasised, for example in bold type, so they stand out.

questions.question: For food sold loose or not prepacked, such as a meal served in a dining room, how may allergen information be provided?
options: Only on the original packaging, which is thrown away.
It does not have to be provided at all.
On a menu, a sign, or given by staff when asked.
Only by writing to the manufacturer.

questions.question: What does the abbreviation PPDS stand for?
options: Packaged perishable dry stock.
Prepared products daily served.
Prepacked for direct sale.
Public protection dietary standard.

questions.question: Since when has Natasha's Law applied to prepacked for direct sale (PPDS) food?
options: It has not yet come into force.
Since 1990.
Since October 2021.
Since 2016.

questions.question: What must PPDS foods now carry under Natasha's Law?
options: No labelling at all, as they are freshly made.
Only the price and a use-by date.
A leaflet describing the kitchen.
A full ingredients list, with the allergens emphasised.

questions.question: If someone asks whether a food is safe for their allergy and you are not certain, what is the best response?
options: Say "let me check", then check the label, care plan or kitchen before answering.
Reassure them it is probably fine to avoid worrying them.
Say nothing and serve it anyway.
Guess based on how the dish looks.

questions.question: Where should you look to find out about a person's allergies before supporting them with food?
options: Your own memory of what they ate last week.
A general guess based on their age.
What seems popular with other people you support.
Their care plan, which should clearly record their allergies and intolerances.

questions.question: A person tells you they react to a food that is not written in their care plan. What should you do?
options: Take it seriously and report it so it can be recorded and assessed.
Wait until they have a reaction before acting.
Ignore it, as only the written plan counts.
Tell them they must be mistaken.

questions.question: What is cross-contamination?
options: When an allergen transfers onto a safe food from another food, a surface, a utensil or unwashed hands.
When a food is cooked for too long.
When two people share a table.
When a label is printed incorrectly.

questions.question: Which habit helps prevent cross-contamination when preparing an allergen-free meal?
options: Assuming a dish is safe if it looks the same as usual.
Storing all foods uncovered together.
Using the same knife for everything without cleaning it.
Using separate utensils, chopping boards and surfaces, or cleaning them thoroughly between foods.

questions.question: Why should you read labels every time, even on a product you have used before?
options: Because labels are required to be read aloud.
Because it is only a legal formality with no safety purpose.
Because the price changes often.
Because recipes can change, so the ingredients may not be the same as last time.

questions.question: How does good allergen care support a person's dignity and choice?
options: By involving them in choices and offering varied, appealing food that is still safe.
By giving them the same few plain meals every day.
By removing their favourite foods entirely.
By deciding all their meals for them without asking.

questions.question: If a person asks for something you know is unsafe for their allergy, what is the right approach?
options: Ignore the request and walk away.
Give it to them to avoid upsetting them.
Tell them off for asking.
Explain kindly, offer a safe alternative, and speak to a senior colleague if needed.

questions.question: Which of these is a milder sign of an allergic reaction?
options: Swelling of the tongue or throat.
Difficulty breathing.
Collapse.
An itchy rash or raised hives.

questions.question: Which of these is a sign that an allergic reaction has become severe?
options: Feeling a little bored at mealtime.
Difficulty breathing, swelling of the tongue or throat, or the person collapsing.
A single sneeze and a slightly runny nose.
A small, temporary itch that quickly settles by itself.

questions.question: A true food allergy involves the immune system and can be life-threatening.
options: True
False

questions.question: A food intolerance involves the immune system and is usually life-threatening.
options: True
False

questions.question: For someone with a serious allergy, even a tiny trace of the food can be enough to trigger a reaction.
options: True
False

questions.question: A reaction that starts off mild will always stay mild, so there is no need to keep watching the person.
options: True
False

questions.question: Antihistamines are the correct treatment for anaphylaxis, so adrenaline is not needed.
options: True
False

questions.question: You should read allergen labels every time, even on a product you have used before, because recipes can change.
options: True
False

questions.question: Even if a person seems to recover after adrenaline, they still need urgent medical attention because symptoms can return.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is fine to leave a person alone once you have called 999 for a severe allergic reaction.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are among the 14 named allergens under UK law? (Select all that apply)
options: Mustard
White sugar
Celery
Table salt
Lupin

questions.question: Which of these are milder signs of an allergic reaction? (Select all that apply)
options: An itchy rash or raised hives
Swelling of the lips, face or eyes
Difficulty breathing
Tingling or itching in the mouth
Collapse

questions.question: Which of these are severe signs that a reaction is affecting breathing or circulation? (Select all that apply)
options: Difficulty breathing or wheezing
Feeling faint, dizzy or collapsing
Swelling of the tongue or throat
A single sneeze
A slightly runny nose

questions.question: Which habits help to avoid cross-contamination? (Select all that apply)
options: Assume a dish is safe if it looks the same as usual
Store foods covered and apart
Use the same knife for peanut butter and an allergy-free sandwich
Wash your hands before handling food and after touching an allergen
Use separate utensils, chopping boards and surfaces, or clean them thoroughly between foods

questions.question: When someone shows signs of anaphylaxis, what should you do? (Select all that apply)
options: Stand the person up suddenly
Cancel the ambulance if they start to feel better
Stay with them the whole time and keep them calm
Call 999 straight away and say you think it is anaphylaxis
Give their adrenaline auto-injector if it is prescribed and their plan says to

questions.question: After an allergic reaction, what should be recorded and reported? (Select all that apply)
options: The signs you saw and the time
What the person ate or was exposed to
Any medication given and how the person responded
Nothing, if the reaction was only mild

questions.question: Which actions support choice and dignity for a person with a food allergy? (Select all that apply)
options: Give them the same few plain meals every day without asking
Find safe versions of favourite meals where you can
Respect their likes, culture and preferences
Involve the person in choosing their food

questions.question: A severe, life-threatening allergic reaction is called ___.
In the UK, food law identifies ___ named allergens that must be declared when used as ingredients.
When an allergen transfers onto a safe food from a surface, utensil or unwashed hands, this is called ___.
If someone shows signs of anaphylaxis, call ___ straight away and say you think it is anaphylaxis.
Natasha's Law applies to food that is prepacked for direct ___.
